Balancing it out so it doesn't spin out of control is an issue for me. But perhaps my nutty brother will give me his healing magnets! hahhaa! Then I can test it out. I'm on such a tight buget that Elon Musk won't even make it to Jupiter without my succeeing. Yes, I am magnificent! :D bah! lol. Still needed, but not the arc de trioomph of space travel, for sure...

author: @dekutree64
text: For 3D printing, I much prefer Blender over proper CAD programs. It's so nice being able to just drag the vertices where you want instead of having to do everything mathematically.

author: @dekutree64
text: Much agreed! Except for the part about always being more difficult than you expect (I have a tendency toward overconfidence). Physical models are so helpful for finding what is and isn't a problem, and sparking new ideas to improve a design.

author: @danielfeyerabend904
text:
So true, good, Doctor!  
I stumbled onto something truly exciting while playing with my grandson.   It was so intriguing that on January 1st, I went rummaging through my junk piles to build a scaled up version from old lawnmowers and a bicycle wheel.  It worked just as I thought!  Since then, I've studied through Grok all the mathematics of why it was doing what I physically saw.  It works out correctly according to the known laws of physics.   Yeah!  Once that step was validated, I have been working out the details of a large prototype.   Your videos of Tinkercad and using a 3d printer were instrumental.   
Hoping to prove the math with the prototype in a few weeks.  
I'm really stuck with using an Arduino, though.  Any assistance there would be wonderfully appreciated.

author: @stewartpalmer2456

author: @abimagery
text:
I ordered a dining table and chairs from an online shipping site. I did not believe in my skills to make chairs. A table I could manage. In the end the whole thing  turned into a logistics disaster waiting over a month for only part of it to arrive and was not what I expected anyway. During the wait, I have learned what it takes to make chairs using steel, which I am familiar using. Today I am building the whole thing from scratch. Sometimes the lazy option is not only not what you want, being mass produced and poor quality, but it does allow for learning out of pure frustration.

What is in the way, becomes the way.

author: @VeniceInventors
text: I've lost count of how many times people came to me with "a great idea" expecting me to figure out how to make it work and build it for them. I found that the biggest red flag is when the motivation behind the idea is to make a lot of money, rather than solve a real problem, which I'd be happy to help with. I also found that the quickest way to bring them back to reality is to ask what their budget is.

author: @PeterGMerchant
text: Some people can visualise things ( I know, a nebulous thing) in their mind and see how they go together. Letting them ferment inside can  move them  around and resolve some problems , without the need for a physical model. My three patents didn't need an actual build, just the ability to write it down.   One of my current ideas is to know the shape of the wings to design my own R/C  controller enclosure in order to 3D print it. It's not quite conical.  I know that once I have the basic design of one side I can duplicate, flip, and rotate to put them together.n

author: @ericblenner-hassett3945
text: Agreed.  One small point everyone overlooks is All Skills are transferable.  Start with drawing. That skill moves to Drafting then into the modern CAD systems.  People just have to learn the new tools to do the same vase with flowers that were in the drawing.  That can move into CAM where it is converted to G Code to carve it out of materials by a machine.  All that from realising drawing is a skill transferred into a method of production.

author: @rochat143
text: Thank you for speaking straight to me. You have been a great source of inspiration to me, teaching me how to think differently. As a mechanic in the Navy and a refrigeration tech, I have a wide range of interests. I have worked with gears two stories tall and gear trains inside a watch. Now that I have lost my functional vision, I am working on a Master's to teach visually impaired people. You have helped me to learn TinkerCAD, get a 3d printer that I promise I will learn how to use, and finish the CVT I have been designing in my head. Thank you .

author: @velcroman11
text:
You are so, so totally wrong.  From 75 years of life experience I can tell you that.  Every time I have had and idea, no matter how crazy or brilliant there was no one to offer me a scintilla of support.  They condemned  me, obstructed me from working on that idea, punished me for my selfish lack of support for what THEY where doing.  Yet, when I did suffer through the slings and arrows of their meanness and succeed.  I was  condemned again!  “You should have done this or that.  I could have done this far better than that.  This is rubbish, it has no value.  All you have done is wasted other peoples time who are actually valuable”.   
I feel you are living the world of Santa Clause and fairies living at the bottom of the garden.  The world is not like as you claim, it is cruel and selfish.  Just remember, I speak with 75 years of miserable, tearful experience.  “Why should I bother with you, why should anyone bother with you.  You are such a waste of space, a problem that just wont go away”.  That; is my life experience and now I am tired.  All I hear now through my tinnitus is the chorus of a life time of “you are stupid, weak, lazy a canker on the people of the word, and of the world itself”.  If I die in my own bed, in my own home, my body will have mummified before anyone notices I’m not around.
I feel you are like the rich man who can not understand the poor.  Oh, so naive are you.  You are blessed.  Stop boasting about it!  I like what you have shown in the past.  I think it is clever.  I’ve never heard those words said to me.  Cherish them.

author: @timmyssweet
text:
Shapr3D is an awesome CAD program - tried an Apple pencils and iPad

For me, for some project, money is the limiting factor, but I sleep on it.  If it's still worth it, reduce the costs as-much-as possible and save for it.

Learning isn't a barrier when you have time.  Perhaps, time is peoples' barrier because they work two jobs and need to relax during their off-time.  I really struggle to comprehend why learning would be a barrier if someone had the time.  Can I suggest that they consider how much time they spend on social media.  Prioritise, and I prioritise by making a list and working towards it.


time - learn - to do list

author: @RogerHyam
text:
Fantasy -> Imagining -> Sketching -> Modelling -> Implementation -> Deployment. 
I struggle to get people from fantasy to imagination. They usually wander off before lifting a finger to do anything grumbling about my negativity!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Yes that first step is pretty tricky to get folks to make

author: @angelusmendez5084
text: That's it mate!

author: @peterling1422
text: Once you build even just a basic cardboard model you can even take photos of it to share with someone who might help you get to the next step. Also fear is so very susceptible to target fixation, if you're riding a bike and only thinking "I better not hit that tree so I will keep my eyes on the tree" you're gonna hit the tree! I think we only need to spend 10-15% of any effort on fears or what "Could" go wrong, it's a pragmatic amount to assess even if I go wrong how wrong can it really go so let's have a plan to reduce impact if it does go wrong. Then we need to spend 85-90% on the thing we're trying to do, if I'm back on the bike and I acknowledge there is a tree there but I spend 85-90% of my time looking towards the empty space where I'm wanting to head I'm going to hit the space where there is NOT a tree! Yay! Rob is so very right, in building a model you might only disappoint yourself, you don't actually have to show or share if it didn't work but you will have observed and learnt both skill (however basic) and about your idea so much more if you'd done nothing. By risking building any model you will also learn possibly your idea isn't as great as it could be but that failure+time=experience then experience+effort = improvement/progress. Repeat this enough and we will all be closer to the inspiration Rob is providing! I've been inspired for the last few years and in about a month I will be excited to share the results of this inspiration 😁😉 this has got to be one of the greatest channels ever, if you are new hear go back through this absolutely amazing library of wisdom and mind-blowing sharing (home made Graphene!!!! Side bare I would like to see what happens if you pop some of that into the resin you 3D print with) keep it up Rob!!!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: That is a great post mate - so much in there to take away - thank you for taking the time to write it - cheers

author: @GavinImpett
text:
I would say time as it applies to real world mortality is what folks are concerned with. Sure, I could learn to fly a jet and given infinite time, earn enough to buy and fly a small one, nothing too fancy, to the UK, for a cup of tea. Or I can go online and pay for use of a seat on a Jet and have Amazon ship me my Barry's Gold. 

When I decided I would like to winter camp in Yosemite in a hot tent, I budgeted one year of time to get started,  eleven months of learning to evaluate, one month of actually getting somewhere. Not everyone can or should put that amount of time or effort into something. Most people never realize time is the deciding factor and come up with other secondary proximate explanations as to why they can't do something.

I am glad to see you are starting to take safety a bit seriously. Although I suspect you cropped in on your grinding video because you weren't wearing the appropriate safety goggles.

author: @OZtwo
text: Yes! if you have an idea then build a model and learn from what you made to make it better!

author: @thatcheapguy525
text:
Robert, if my teenage self some 45ish years ago could have had access to a mentor like yourself my life would have taken a very different direction. some of the seemingly crazy ideas I had back then would have surely found a way of being modelled/prototyped. Dean Kamen had similar thinking to one of them and the basic principle of which we know today as the Segway. my thinking came from observing the gyroscopic effect of Scalextric car motors when experimenting with them to tune for more performance. I discussed the idea of 'tuning' electric motors and their gyroscopic effect with our head of Physics at school who promptly put down any such notion that an electric motor could be tuned and that its gyroscopic effect was nothing more than a hinderance. well, I had no idea what I was doing but after much trial and error with wire gauges & turns, permanent magnet strength & distancing etc my cars were lapping way faster than anyone else. the Segway type thing remained as a group of sketches and text found recently when going through a load of old childhood stuff at my mums house.
back then EVERYTHING was incredibly expensive and money was scarce. nowadays my equivalent teenage self can access a mountain of physical resources very cheaply and have free access to people like yourself showing how its done AND providing motivational/inspirational videos. crazy ideas really can be brought to life.

author: @ruthcormack1765
text: Break the build down into stages & always ensure you have something workable at each stage to build onto. And maybe some nice music. The in - between stages will give you a chance to consider improvements from the original design.

author: @CrudelyMade
text:
part of "helping" is saying "to handle the next few steps you have to suffer through some learning"   it can also be helpful to say "at this point I can either do it for you or I can guide you, but I can only help you if you have some skills. it's like building a house. I can do it for you, but if you have no skills, I can't help you do it.  I want to help, but you need to be able to do some or most of the work.  so if you have a passion for your idea, you'll spend some time learning to do some things, and then you can reach out to people who can help you.  otherwise you're just looking for someone else to do it for you."    part of our societal problems is people have forgotten the meanings of words. 
many people think 'help' is someone doing it for you while they supervise or do something else. 
it's almost like speaking a different language with very similar sounding words.

author: @EgonSorensen
text:
I agree - it is easy to go from the real world view(s) and understanding(s) to the theory-world-model-building,
The hard hurdle is trying to get those invested into the established science to be open to the real-world-theory - job security, I suppose ;ø)
So, finding a practical thing - model - etc. that demonstrates and supports your work is key to get others interested and open up their mind(s) 
Funny how this applies to virtually every aspect of reality - thanks Robert!

text: What really helped me break the barrier which is mental. You're so right it's fear. A silent fear. I call this being buffaloed. Is art which I found I was good at. First I see the picture in my mind and then I just start. I find the hardest part for me was just starting. where to start buffaloed me. Silly I know. But it help me break the mental barrier that really is a prison to your dreams and  ambition therefore your personal freedom. I like to have never stated on the weirding modules which are like batterys but not. They only reservoir not store. So I hope I've said something that makes sense to someone. Baby steps start slow and enjoy the moment. I like this ancient saying. The trying of our faith, brings forth patience in us. Patience in us practiced brings forth experience. Experience in us applied.  Brings forth hope in us. And hope my friends is the mother of all victories

author: @mickeyfilmer5551
text: I bought a small 3d printer (malyan 200 for £120) and was clueless about how to do anything with it, so a few YouTube videos and suggestions to use Tinkercad ,then I found your channel with a mahooosive mine of information and tutorials on 3d printing. I'm a similar age to you and my bandwidth aint wot it used to be, but hey ho I now have a creality big 3d printer and make lots of things. (with a big thanks to you!!) so no excuses out there... If I can do it literally anyone can!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice one mate - thanks for sharing that

author: @mitchpayson6203
text: Thanks for taking the time. It really is fear and confidence or a lack there of that holds back many of us. Time is so very precious. We appreciate yours. For those who've  taken their first step, look ahead, keep yourself balanced, and momentum will come quickly. Good luck 😊

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: It is surprising when you look back how far you have come!

author: @TheTrumanZoo
text: words of wisdom, thank you.

author: @duvelr
text: Thank you so much! I honestly believe this beautiful lesson can be generalized far beyond the realm of invention/creation, It can possibly be applied to every human endeavor.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you may well be right mate - cheers

author: @Fr333man
text: Thank you Robert! I think you have helped 1000’s perhaps millions of individuals build backyard projects! You are responsible for thousands of husbands having big elephant projects cover their backyards and garages 😂 certainly mine 😣 In any case the proof of concept is the beginning stage of development, I have built countless projects with few working properly but all giving me success in understanding 💪 thank you for that 🙏

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is so awesome cheers lol

author: @oieieio741
text: Nice video, Rob — so true. I also wanted to share something a young person at a recent hackathon told me. She said she rarely, if ever, thinks of herself as “failing.” Instead, she prefers to call it “falling,” because it makes more sense to her. I fall, and I get back up — I rarely fail.💪💫

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - that is a great attitude and a great way to say it

author: @andy1way
text: Very sound advice. Top man.😊

author: @tonysummers2334
text: Oh so true.

author: @billschwandt1
text: When you checked out my magnet windmill, you really inspired me. I've built several prototypes and just received my first 3d printer because you convinced me I MUST learn it. I'm buying a motorcycle and have bought thousands of dollars of special magnets for what I'm about to do with my spin lab. I watch your videos and have heard you loud and clear. Just wait till you see what's next, sir! 🫡

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I am looking forward to seeing it mate

author: @MrNatural451
text: LOL!!! While I'm listening to this, I'm cutting, stripping and rearranging wires to try and figure out why the simple switch on the air filter I'm building isn't working. Very apropos.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - I didn't say it wouldn't be frustrating mate lol - been exactly where you are

author: @Fingerlords
text: Cool

text: GO GO GO !!! 😊
